annotations.text: The olfactory hallucinations suggest a focal seizure arising in the uncus.
The mass in the brain may be neoplastic or nonneoplastic in origin.
the presence of calcification and the prepontine lesion, which suggests meningeal involvement, a unifying diagnosis could be cerebral sarcoidosis.
the absence of the sicca syndrome. The patient is black, and sarcoidosis is more common among black persons than among white persons.

Tumefactive, enhancing nonneoplastic brain lesions can be caused by inflammatory diseases, such as multiple sclerosis, acute disseminated encephalomyelitis, sarcoidosis, Behçet’s disease with neurologic involvement, tuberculosis, fungal infections, and syphilitic gumma.

When a neurologic syndrome is the only clinically detectable abnormality, as was the case in this patient, high-resolution PET-CT of the chest, abdomen, and neck should be performed because it is sensitive in detecting extraneural lesions, most often in the mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes or the lungs.

the results of magnetic resonance spectroscopy were mostly unhelpful, although they indicated that the nonspecific findings are not consistent with a diagnosis of neoplasia but do not entirely rule it out.
The gross lesion did not have the infiltrative character that I had expected.
Granulomas appeared as solitary small lesions in the brain parenchyma that elicited a mild gliotic reaction and were predominantly perivascular.
The immunosuppressive drugs may slow or stop progression but cannot cure these diseases.
In this case, if we had done the chest CT, would we have obtained a sample of that lymph node?
I think that we would have been obligated to confirm the diagnosis with a biopsy either way.
